entry.title: New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live
entry.content:
TLDR: The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey is now live on learning.sap and alongside it the New Role Play based SAP Certified Data Architect certification is now available in this learning Journey: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect

Early in my career, I sat in a room full of architects debating a major platform decision. The conversation covered integration patterns, application landscapes, and total cost of ownership. No one discussed the data. Not the quality of it, not how it would flow, not how it would be governed. We treated data as the responsibility of someone else (master data team and BW team) in a different meeting. Years later, we are seeing AI initiatives slow down because of untrustworthy data, I understand exactly what was missing from that room.

Data architecture has spent years as a supporting discipline important, but rarely the headline. That has changed. The shift to agentic AI has moved data architecture from a back-office concern to a front-line competitive capability.

Agentic AI systems do not simply retrieve information. They reason, plan, and act. They require data that is real-time, contextually grounded, semantically rich, and governed with precision. An agent operating on stale, fragmented, or poorly modelled data does not just produce mediocre outputs it can produce incorrect actions. At enterprise scale, that is a governance and business risk problem, not just a technical one.

The architects who will lead in this environment are those who can hold both conversations at once: the strategic (how data drives business value) and the technical (how to design the platforms, integration patterns, and governance structures that make that value real).

That is exactly what we have tried to address with the New Data Architecture learning Course (Advanced) and a New Data Architect Certification from SAP

Introducing the Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Course

 

The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey (course BDCDA2) is now live on learning.sap.com. Some of you might have done ‘Becoming a Data Architect – Foundational Course’ which was launched last year and this new course takes a deeper look at the topics related to Data Architecture.

Across four-unit journey designed for architects who are ready to move beyond foundational concepts and into the architecture decisions, framework synthesis, and platform-specific depth that define effective data leadership in the agentic AI era.

  1.  Unit 1: Architecting Data Strategy for the Agentic AI Era

The journey opens with strategy, because that is where most data initiatives fail first. Unit 1 builds the five foundational pillars of data strategy: Governance, Data literacy, Stewardship, Architecture, and Technology enablement and connects each directly to AI readiness. It examines what agentic AI demands from data pipelines that traditional analytics architectures were not built to provide: real-time streaming, semantic enrichment, and contextual grounding. It then moves to leadership and culture, the organizational factors that determine whether a data strategy survives contact with reality and closes with a framework for measuring data strategy success and analyzing real-world value patterns.

  1. Unit 2: Integrating Frameworks for Data Architecture

Architecture without a framework produces inconsistency. Unit 2 examines the three frameworks that define the professional standard in this field: TOGAF, DAMA-DMBOK, and the SAP Enterprise Architecture Framework and, guides how to synthesize them rather than apply them in isolation. It then applies that synthesis to two of the most significant architectural paradigms in the market today: Data Mesh and Data Fabric.

  1.  Unit 3: Harmonizing SAP Business Data Cloud Within the Open Data Ecosystem

Unit 3 is where strategy and frameworks meet the SAP platform. It begins with BDC Connect and the Delta Sharing protocol the mechanism that enables zero-copy data sharing across ecosystem boundaries and then works through each major integration scenario in the open data ecosystem: Databricks, Snowflake (including all three connectivity options), Microsoft Fabric, and Google Big Query. The unit closes with a dedicated lesson on data federation performance optimization the practical trade-offs between federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing that every architect advising on SAP Business Data Cloud deployments needs to understand cold.

  1. Unit 4: Designing Advanced Data Models and Architecture for SAP Business Data Cloud

The final unit addresses SAP Business Data Cloud as a Business Data Fabric how it implements federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing to connect heterogeneous data sources while preserving business context and semantic coherence. It covers Data Mesh in SAP BDC, including how Datasphere Spaces implement the four Data Mesh principles in practice. It then moves to one of the most consequential topics in enterprise AI architecture: semantic models and knowledge graphs as the foundation for trustworthy AI and RAG-based systems.

 Introducing the SAP Certified Data Architect Certification

Alongside the learning journey, we have launched the SAP Certified Data Architect certification.

The SAP Certified Data Architect is an AI role-play-based certification that assesses how well candidates understand modern data architecture in the context of strategic business success. It covers the five major architectural paradigms: Data Warehouse, Data Lake, Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Data Lakehouse, alongside the core elements of data maturity: governance frameworks, data security, and business-aligned data strategy. It uses interactive question-and-answer format, assessing the understanding of SAP Data Architecture in the agentic AI era specifically the ability to synthesize and articulate how SAP Business Data Cloud enables governed, semantically coherent data access across hybrid cloud environments.

Note: It is recommended that the candidate completes the 1) Becoming an SAP Data Architect and 2) Mastering SAP Data Architecture courses before attempting the certificate to ensure successful completion.

 Who This Is For

The learning journey and certification are designed for practitioners who are already working in the data and architecture space and want to develop the rigor, depth, and formal recognition to operate at senior level. That includes data architects designing enterprise data platforms, enterprise architects expanding their data architecture competency, analytics and BI leaders responsible for governed data access, and technical decision-makers advising customers on SAP Business Data Cloud strategy.

 If you are starting your data architecture journey, the foundational 'Becoming a Data Architect' learning journey on learning.sap.com is the right starting point. The advanced journey picks up where that one ends.

Getting Started

  1. The Becoming a Certified SAP Data Architect Learning Journey live now for both internal and external learners: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect
  2. SAP Certified Data Architect certification registration: https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-data-architect-sap-business-data-cloud

A Final Thought

In the agentic AI era, the constraint on enterprise intelligence is rarely the algorithm. It is the architecture underneath it the decisions made about how data is structured, governed, connected, and made meaningful. Those decisions require a specific set of skills. This learning journey and certification exist to build them.

The practitioners who invest in that depth now will be the ones designing the data foundations that AI-enabled enterprises run on. That work is not infrastructure. It is strategy.

Happy Learning!

entry.title: Create a Custom SAP BDC Data Product share it with Enterprise Databricks, extend it and send it back
entry.content:
Overview:

This blog demonstrates how to leverage SAP Business Data Catalog (BDC) in conjunction with Enterprise Databricks to create custom data products, extend them with advanced analytics and transformations, and synchronize the refined data back to SAP BDC. This integrated approach enables organizations to maximize data value while maintaining governance and traceability across their data infrastructure.

Main steps at a glance

1. Check the prerequisites

a. Enable your SAP BDC Core in SAP4Me portal, it important to provision it in the same hyperscaler and region of your Enterprise Databricks or in the future your GCP or Microsoft Fabric tenants, because you may incur some egress fees when you share data products through a BDC Connect instance. You can refer to this blog Introductory Guide to Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud

b. Provision your SAP Datasphere tenat in SAP BDC through SAP4Me you can refer to this blog Provisioning of Business Data Cloud : SAP Datasphere  when you provision at least allocate 4,150 CU because you need to activate object store in you tenant, so you need to increase the storage to 384 GB and memory to 128 GB and in the object store you need to add 1 block for storage, 1 block for memory and 1 block for requests you can use the SAP Business Data Cloud Capacity Unit Estimator to configure your business case

c. Create one HANA Data Lake Files space in you Datasphere tenant, and add it to an scope role to provide access

d. Provision your SAP BDC Connect instance in SAP4Me portal, you can check this blog How to provision SAP BDC Connect for Databricks?

e. Prepare your source system with all the configuration needed to connect with you SAP Datasphere tenant, in this document you can check all the connection types and prerequisites -> SAP Datasphere Connection Types

f. If you want to connect an S4HANA system then you need to configure your cloud connector for S4HANA PCE or S4HANA On-Premise connection with SAP Datasphere:

  • Step 1: Setup SAP Cloud Connector connect to SAPDatasphere
  • Step 2: Setup mapping (RFC connection) in Cloud Connector for a SAP S/4HANA On-Premise connection
  • Step 3: Create SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Connection for Data Flow and Replication Flow
  • Step 4: Replication Flow for CDS views from S/4HANA On-Premise to SAP Datasphere

2. Create the formation

a. Create your formation in SAP4Me portal. Go to Portfolio & Products -> My Product Packages tab-> Business Data Cloud product package -> Customer Landscape tab -> Formations sub-tab and choose Create Formation, enter a unique formation name and choose Integration with SAP Business Data Cloud

b. Include the following mandatory systems: SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere and SAP BDC Connect instance. It's not mandatory to include your source system in the formation

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

3. Create custom managed data product

a. Open your SAP Datasphere tenant. First of all replicate your data. You can replicate directly to object store creating a connection from your source system to SAP Datasphere in the HANA Data Lake Files space or you can replicate an existing table coming from another space in your Datasphere tenant. If you want to use a view or analytic model you must materialize the view or model in a table, then replicate into a table in your HANA Data Lake Files space. My table is TL_ADW_OBSESSION

b. Create a formation profile in your SAP Datasphere tenant. Go to Data Sharing Cockpit -> My Data Provider Profile -> Create Data Provider Profile. In this example my data provider profile is ONB_Demo

 

c. Go to Data Sharing Cockpit -> My Data Products -> Click on Create Product (+ icon)

d. In Artifact Space select the object store space for your data products

e. Select your table in my case is TL_ADW_OBSESSION and click on Save Changes

f. Click on Switch Status and select List. When the data product is Listed please open it

g. Open your SAP Business Data Cloud Cockpit and go to Catalog & Marketplace, click on Search and we look for ONB_DEMO data product, open it and click on Share

h. Click on Add Target. Select your BDC Connect instance and click on Share

4. Extend and push it back to SAP BDC

a. In our SAP Datasphere tenant we select the space which can install data products. Go to System -> Configuration -> Business Data Products

b. Select your BDC Connect Instance and in the left side click on Add to select the space where you want to install data products

c. Open your Enterprise Databricks workspace and go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing

d. Click on your BDC Connect provider

e. Click on Mount to Catalog

f. Provide the catalog name and click on create

                                 

 

 

 

 

g. Go back to your Catalog and in Delta Shares Received you can see bdc_deltashare catalog with the table coming from Datasphere TL_ADW_OBSESSION

h. Extend the table with advanced analytics and/or transformations. Then new table in Databricks is cluster_final, the next step is create a share object. Go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing. Click on Shared by me tab and click Share data

i. Create a share page, enter the share Name and add an optional comment. Click on Save and continue

j. On the Add data assets tab, select the data assets you want to share. In this case I want to share the view cluster_final. Then click on Save and continue

k. If is the first time you share assets maybe the system will ask to grant some required permissions, click on Confirm

l. In the next step select the notebooks you want to share. In this case I don't want it. Click on Save and continue

m. In the final step select your BDC Connect provider as recipient. Then click on Share data

n. Now we have a share bdc_connect with this we are sharing the view cluster_final

o. Then please go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing. Click on Shared by me tab, click Recipients and select the recipient

p. Click on Grant share. On the Grant share dialog, start typing the share name or click the drop-down menu to select the shares you want to grant, finally click on Grant

q. Finally you need to publishing the share as a Data Product to SAP Business Data Cloud. The next image is an example about the last step to publish, please follow this documentation -> Publishing the Delta Share as a Data Product to SAP Business Data Cloud.  for the complete process and steps

r. Then we come back to SAP BDC Cockpit and we should be able to see our new data product coming from Enterprise Databricks

Summary:

In this blog, we explore how to create a custom data product in SAP Datasphere, the steps to share it with an SAP BDC Connect instance, extend it in Databrick by creating a new cluster table and finally, send it back to SAP Business Data Cloud as a new data product.

entry.title: AI Needs Clean Data. Business Networks Stop It Getting Dirty in the First Place
entry.content:
There is a concept in professional kitchens called mise en place, everything in its place before service begins. The best chefs do not start cooking and hope the ingredients sort themselves out mid-order. The prep happens first, deliberately and completely, so that when the pressure is on the kitchen can move at pace without chaos.

For some reason my brain decided to align that principle to the concept of data quality and its use in agentic AI. I finally figured out what caused the spark, Zero100's AI-Ready Data Imperative report which defines the material difference between data that informs a decision, data that triggers a recommendation, and data that an agent acts on autonomously (there is also a nice podcast on the topic). A dashboard built on imperfect data gets questioned by a human who applies judgement and context. An agent built on imperfect data acts, reroutes the shipment, adjusts the replenishment signal, commits to the supplier, before anyone has noticed the input was wrong. The same ambiguity that a person absorbs in a meeting becomes operational risk the moment a system is doing the acting.

The same research found that only 15% of organisations consider their data foundations execution-ready. Let that land for a moment, because that means 85% of the AI programmes being announced, funded, and celebrated right now are being built on ground that cannot support the weight being placed on them.

The honest reality is that the foundations have not been prepared for this level of trust, and most organisations are not yet having that conversation clearly enough to know it.

The instinctive response is to treat this as a reason to delay. Clean everything first, then deploy agents. That instinct is understandable, but most organisations do not have the luxury of acting on it. Board pressure is intense, Wall Street is rewarding AI narratives and punishing silence, and vendors are racing to rebadge everything as agentic. The result is a top-down mandate to move fast colliding with a bottom-up reality that the foundations are not ready. Waiting for the data estate to be fully clean is not a strategy, it is an indefinite deferral dressed up as diligence. I have yet to meet the enterprise that finished a data quality programme and declared victory.

So the real question is not how do we clean everything but what do we actually need to clean to make a specific decision trustworthy.

Zero100's research consistently finds that the list of data inputs that actually determine the outcome of any given decision is shorter than most people expect. Work backwards from the decision, inventory rebalancing, late shipment triage, supplier payment timing, and ask which fields, if wrong or late, make the agent's action wrong or late. Typically ten to twenty fields. They call these Golden Inputs, not because they are glamorous, but because they carry disproportionate weight and deserve disproportionate attention. Govern those fields with the same rigour applied to operational KPIs, and you have a foundation an agent can genuinely act on. Leave them in the same state as everything else, and you have an agent making fast, confident, wrong decisions.

Here is where the argument takes a turn that most conversations about data readiness miss entirely. In supply chain, the triggering event is almost always external. A supplier confirmation, a carrier delay, an invoice exception, a forecast revision. The decisions agents will be asked to make are not primarily driven by data that originates inside your four walls. They are driven by signals arriving from outside them. Which means the most consequential Golden Inputs in any supply chain decision are very often external data fields, not internal ones.

This matters because it reframes the entire data cleansing challenge. Internal data cleansing is valuable but it is a continuous battle, because dirty data keeps entering the estate faster than it can be cleaned. Every new transaction, every supplier update, every manual entry is a potential source of contamination. It is the culinary equivalent of trying to keep a kitchen clean while unchecked deliveries arrive throughout service.

Business networks change that dynamic fundamentally. Data arriving over a governed network is structured, validated, and clean at the point of entry. Those fields do not join the cleansing backlog because they never needed cleansing in the first place. Think of it as the difference between a kitchen that inspects every ingredient as it arrives from the supplier, and one that waits until service to discover the fish was not fresh. Quality is established before it enters the kitchen, not discovered as a problem halfway through cooking.

This is where SAP Business Network plays a defining role. With millions of trading partners already connected and transacting through structured, governed document flows, the network is delivering AI-ready Golden Inputs at scale today, not as a future state. Purchase orders, advance ship notices, invoices, confirmations, each carrying their critical fields, all arriving clean by design. For organisations building toward agentic AI in supply chain, that is not a nice-to-have, it is the shortest path between ambition and trustworthy autonomous action.

The path to agentic AI is not a transformation programme. It is a sequence of small, governed steps, each one earning the right to the next. Start with a single decision, identify its Golden Inputs, and rather than beginning an internal cleansing project of uncertain duration, ask which of those inputs arrives via a business network and is therefore already clean, already structured, already trustworthy. In supply chain that answer is often most of them.

The problem in most kitchens is not the quality of the chefs. It is that the ingredients keep arriving without quality checks, and the team spends half its time sorting through the delivery before it can start cooking. The best kitchens do not just prep well, they have rigorous supplier standards, so the ingredients arrive ready to use.

That is what business networks do for agentic AI. Not a better mop. A cleaner kitchen.

entry.title: New Webinar Series: Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise - for Architects, by Architects
entry.content:
Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise: A Webinar Series for Architects, by Architects

TLDR; An 11-part webinar series unpacking SAP's Autonomous Enterprise for Architects covering Joule Work, the Autonomous Suite, the Business AI Platform, Knowledge Graph, AI agents, and the SAP Family of Models and what it all means for architects!

Starting: 1st June to 12 August 2026, weekly, 17:00–18:00 Europe / 20:30–21:30 India / 11:00–12:00 New York / 08:00–09:00 Palo Alto Time.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise: A Webinar Series for Architects, by Architects

Architecture is entering a new era. AI is moving beyond isolated copilots and individual experiments toward enterprise-wide orchestration, automation, and decision intelligence. That shift is not incremental it changes how enterprises are designed, governed, and operated in an AI-native world, and it places architects squarely at the centre of the conversation.

Following the latest announcements from Sapphire, we are pleased to launch Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise is an 11-part webinar series built for this moment.

Designed by architects, for architects, the series unpacks SAP's vision for the Autonomous Enterprise and translates it into the architectural choices, governance considerations, and adoption patterns that practitioners will actually have to make.

The announcements from SAP Sapphire have moved the conversation about enterprise AI from possibility to architecture. Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite, the SAP Business AI Platform, Knowledge Graph, AI agents, and the SAP Family of Models are no longer roadmap items. They are concrete capabilities that architects will be expected to design with, govern, secure, operationalise, and scale.

With this series, we aim to provide architect's a clear view of how all parts of an Autonomous Enterprise fit together, where the design decisions actually sit, and what changes in the role of the architect when AI agents become first-class citizens in the enterprise landscape.

This series is structured to close that gap, taking participants from the strategic Why through the architectural and technical How, without skipping the messy parts in between.

What makes this series different

This is not a product walkthrough. Each session is led by practitioners, product leaders, customers or guest speakers who are building, governing, or advising customers on the same architectural choices you are working through.

The format prioritises depth over breadth: one focused topic per session, with room for real Q&A.

The series brings together SAP architects, product teams, customers, and external industry experts including a customer perspectives on SAP Family of Models, View on data foundations, and an external governance perspective from Steve Tuppen of Mozaic, alongside a keynote from Dr. Sebastian Wernicke, author of Data Inspired and TED speaker. We also have SAP Leaders such as Andre Christ and Mark Smith joining to share their strategic PoV on the topic!

The series runs from 1 June to 12 August 2026, with weekly one-hour sessions.

All sessions are held at 17:00–18:00 Europe / 20:30–21:30 India / 11:00–12:00 New York / 08:00–09:00 Palo Alto time zones.

 The Session Details Below:

# Session Title Speakers Date

1Unpacking SAP's Autonomous Enterprise for ArchitectsPaul Kurchina, Mark Smith, Gaurish Dessai, Jason Luo1 June 2026
2What SAP's Evolved Portfolio Means for ArchitectsPaul Kurchina, Ralph Richter, Surprise Speaker 🙂10 June 2026
3Architecting From Intent to Action with Joule Work & Knowledge GraphJason Taylor & John Santic16 June 2026
4Playbook for Building Your Own SAP AgentsJulia Kloppenburg, Gaurish Dessai26 June 2026
5From Patterns to Consumption: SAP's Innovation Factory for Architecting and Activating AI in SAP LandscapesHelmut Grimm, Raghu Banda, Jose Tornero, Vishal Mehta1 July 2026
6An Architect's Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management at Enterprise ScaleAndré Christ, Laurenz Vogt8 July 2026
7An Architect's Guide to the SAP Family of ModelsMarkus Kohler, Simon Knehr , Ashok Veilumuthu14 July 2026
8SAP's North Star Architecture and Golden AI PathAnirban Majumdar16th July *
9Data Foundations for an Autonomous EnterpriseNiclas Schlautkoetter, Dr. Sebastian Wernicke, Surprise Customer Speaker 22 July 2026
10From Autonomous Domains to Industry AI: One Platform, Two LensesRaghu Banda, Naveen Dumpa, Cristina Dan29 July 2026
11AI Governance and Security for an Autonomous EnterpriseSteve Tuppen (Mozaic), Dr. Michael . Blaschke, Stephan Wronkowski-Elster5 August 2026
12Role of an Enterprise Architect in Driving the Autonomous EnterprisePaul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch, Johannes Euler12 August 2026
13

The Human-Agent Orchestrator: Leading, Governing, and Scaling the Autonomous Enterprise

Surprise Guest Speaker20th Aug 2026

* Exact Timing of the session to be confirmed soon.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

How the series is structured?

The 11 sessions are sequenced deliberately.

The first three sessions establish the foundation. Session 1 unpacks the Autonomous Enterprise concept fresh from the Sapphire announcements and introduces the key components Joule Work, the Autonomous Suite, the Business AI Platform, and the SAP Family of Models. Session 2 examines how the full SAP portfolio comes together as a continuous, coherent capability rather than a one-time initiative, including the North Star and Golden Path AI framing. Session 3 then goes deep on Joule Work and the Knowledge Graph as the dynamic engagement layer that interprets intent, surfaces insights, and orchestrates AI agents across the enterprise.

Session 4 shifts from concept to construction. It is a hands-on playbook for building an SAP agent from scratch a show-and-tell session intended as a working reference for architects who need to demonstrate the stack to their own teams.

Sessions 5 through 9 go deeper into the architectural layers that matter most. Session 5 introduces SAP's Innovation Factory and the three connected threads of architecture patterns and persona-based discovery, the Innovation Factory as a repeatable approach, and the activation-to-consumption playbook. Session 6 tackles AI agent lifecycle management at enterprise scale how to discover, govern, secure, and measure the business impact of hundreds or thousands of autonomous agents without introducing agent sprawl, architectural drift, or unmanaged risk. Session 7 goes deep on the SAP Family of Models, including a customer example.

Session 8 brings together Dr. Sebastian Wernicke, Niclas Schlautkoetter on the role of the Data Architect and what is new with BDC, and a customer perspective anchored by the principle that the difference between isolated pilots and sustainable AI at scale lies in the foundational data architecture.

Session 9 closes this arc with autonomous domains organised around Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, HCM, and Customer Engagement, alongside Reference Industry AI Kits that accelerate transformations with pre-built SAP best practice content.

The final two sessions close the loop. Session 10 brings in external perspective from Steve Tuppen of Mozaic on AI governance and security, alongside SAP experts on privacy, compliance, trust, clean-core principles, and interoperability across SAP and non-SAP environments defining guardrails for AI solutions that are effective, scalable, and governable.

Session 11 returns to the role itself: what the Autonomous Enterprise means for Enterprise Architects, how EAs architect decision-making across the enterprise using AI, and a wrap-up of highlights and next steps across the series together with Paul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch and Johannes Euler.

Why this matters for every architect discipline

The Autonomous Enterprise is not a single product decision. It is an architectural one, and it cuts across every discipline in the architect community. Enterprise Architects need a defensible view of how AI changes decision-making, value streams, and the operating model and how to govern that change without slowing it down.

Solution and Platform Architects need to know where Joule Work, agents, and the SAP Family of Models actually sit in a design, what they replace, and what they expose. Integration Architects are facing a landscape where agents and MCP servers are becoming first-class consumers and producers across the integration fabric, with real implications for orchestration, identity, and observability. Data and AI Architects sit at the centre of it all without a deliberate data foundation, AI initiatives stall in pilot mode regardless of how strong the application layer becomes. This series brings the four perspectives into the same conversation, in the same sequence, so the architectural picture lines up across roles rather than being assembled in fragments later.

Who should attend?

This series is designed for professionals shaping the future of enterprise transformation in an AI-native world:

  • Enterprise Architects driving business and technology transformation
  • Solution and Platform Architects modernising SAP landscapes and integration strategies
  • Data and AI Architects designing the foundations for intelligent, autonomous operations
  • Technology and Transformation Leaders responsible for AI adoption and innovation strategy
  • SAP Customers and Partners exploring how to operationalise AI across enterprise landscapes
  • Architecture and Governance Teams focused on scalability, security, compliance, and lifecycle management
  • Innovation and Business Technology Teams moving from AI pilots to enterprise-wide adoption

As the role of the architect becomes increasingly multidimensional spanning business strategy, data, AI, governance, platforms, security, and human-centric transformation this series is built to help architects navigate and lead across these interconnected domains with confidence.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

What you will take away

By the end of the series, you will have:

  • A working mental model of how Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite, the Business AI Platform, Knowledge Graph, and the SAP Family of Models fit together architecturally
  • A hands-on reference for building, governing, and observing SAP agents across the enterprise
  • A clearer view of the data and governance foundations that determine whether AI scales or stalls
  • Customer-grounded perspectives from organisations including Bayer and Henkel on what is actually working in production
  • A holistic view of how the Enterprise Architect's role evolves when AI agents become first-class citizens within the enterprise landscape

We strongly recommend attending live where possible. The Q&A in each session is where the most useful exchanges tend to happen, and the cohort across the series will include architects from across the SAP ecosystem customers, partners, and SAP experts.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

Architects will define what the Autonomous Enterprise actually looks like in production. This series is built to make sure that definition is a deliberate one.

We look forward to seeing you in the sessions.

A Big Thank you to all the collaborators and my co-leads for this series!

 @JaSoN_Luo , @ralph_richter , @PaulKurchina @VenkataRaghu_Banda @helmut_grimm @johanneseuler

entry.title: Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise: A Webinar Series for Architects, by Architects
entry.content:
TLDR; An 11-part webinar series unpacking SAP's Autonomous Enterprise for Architects covering Joule Work, the Autonomous Suite, the Business AI Platform, Knowledge Graph, AI agents, and the SAP Family of Models and what it all means for architects!

Starting: 1st June to 12 August 2026, weekly, 17:00–18:00 Europe / 20:30–21:30 India / 11:00–12:00 New York / 08:00–09:00 Palo Alto Time.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

Architecture is entering a new era. AI is moving beyond isolated copilots and individual experiments toward enterprise-wide orchestration, automation, and decision intelligence. That shift is not incremental it changes how enterprises are designed, governed, and operated in an AI-native world, and it places architects squarely at the centre of the conversation.

Following the latest announcements from Sapphire, we are pleased to launch Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise is an 11-part webinar series built for this moment.

 The Session Details Below:

No.Session TitleSpeakersDate
1Unpacking SAP's Autonomous Enterprise for ArchitectsPaul Kurchina, Mark Smith, Gaurish Dessai, Jason Luo1 June 2026
2What SAP's Evolved Portfolio Means for ArchitectsPaul Kurchina, Ralph Richter, Surprise Speaker 🙂10 June 2026
3Architecting From Intent to Action with Joule Work & Knowledge GraphJacob Hornberger, Pavithra GK16 June 2026*
4Playbook for Building Your Own SAP AgentsJulia Kloppenburg, Gaurish Dessai26 June 2026
5From Patterns to Consumption: SAP's Innovation Factory for Architecting and Activating AI in SAP LandscapesHelmut Grimm, Raghu Banda, Jose Tornero, Vishal Mehta1 July 2026
6An Architect's Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management at Enterprise ScaleAndré Christ, Laurenz Vogt8 July 2026
7An Architect's Guide to the SAP Family of ModelsMarkus Kohler, Simon Knehr , Ashok Veilumuthu14 July 2026
8Data Foundations for an Autonomous EnterpriseNiclas Schlautkoetter, Dr. Sebastian Wernicke, Surprise Customer Speaker 22 July 2026
9From Autonomous Domains to Industry AI: One Platform, Two LensesRaghu Banda, Naveen Dumpa, Cristina Dan29 July 2026
10AI Governance and Security for an Autonomous EnterpriseSteve Tuppen (Mozaic), Dr. Michael . Blaschke, Stephan Wronkowski-Elster5 August 2026
11Role of an Enterprise Architect in Driving the Autonomous EnterprisePaul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch, Johannes Euler12 August 2026

* To be Finalised

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

How the series is structured?

The 11 sessions are sequenced deliberately.

The first three sessions establish the foundation. Session 1 unpacks the Autonomous Enterprise concept fresh from the Sapphire announcements and introduces the key components Joule Work, the Autonomous Suite, the Business AI Platform, and the SAP Family of Models. Session 2 examines how the full SAP portfolio comes together as a continuous, coherent capability rather than a one-time initiative, including the North Star and Golden Path AI framing. Session 3 then goes deep on Joule Work and the Knowledge Graph as the dynamic engagement layer that interprets intent, surfaces insights, and orchestrates AI agents across the enterprise.

Session 4 shifts from concept to construction. It is a hands-on playbook for building an SAP agent from scratch a show-and-tell session intended as a working reference for architects who need to demonstrate the stack to their own teams.

Sessions 5 through 9 go deeper into the architectural layers that matter most. Session 5 introduces SAP's Innovation Factory and the three connected threads of architecture patterns and persona-based discovery, the Innovation Factory as a repeatable approach, and the activation-to-consumption playbook. Session 6 tackles AI agent lifecycle management at enterprise scale how to discover, govern, secure, and measure the business impact of hundreds or thousands of autonomous agents without introducing agent sprawl, architectural drift, or unmanaged risk. Session 7 goes deep on the SAP Family of Models, including a customer example.

Session 8 brings together Dr. Sebastian Wernicke, Niclas Schlautkoetter on the role of the Data Architect and what is new with BDC, and a customer perspective anchored by the principle that the difference between isolated pilots and sustainable AI at scale lies in the foundational data architecture.

Session 9 closes this arc with autonomous domains organised around Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, HCM, and Customer Engagement, alongside Reference Industry AI Kits that accelerate transformations with pre-built SAP best practice content.

The final two sessions close the loop. Session 10 brings in external perspective from Steve Tuppen of Mozaic on AI governance and security, alongside SAP experts on privacy, compliance, trust, clean-core principles, and interoperability across SAP and non-SAP environments defining guardrails for AI solutions that are effective, scalable, and governable.

Session 11 returns to the role itself: what the Autonomous Enterprise means for Enterprise Architects, how EAs architect decision-making across the enterprise using AI, and a wrap-up of highlights and next steps across the series together with Paul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch and Johannes Euler.

Why this matters for every architect discipline

The Autonomous Enterprise is not a single product decision. It is an architectural one, and it cuts across every discipline in the architect community. Enterprise Architects need a defensible view of how AI changes decision-making, value streams, and the operating model and how to govern that change without slowing it down.

Solution and Platform Architects need to know where Joule Work, agents, and the SAP Family of Models actually sit in a design, what they replace, and what they expose. Integration Architects are facing a landscape where agents and MCP servers are becoming first-class consumers and producers across the integration fabric, with real implications for orchestration, identity, and observability. Data and AI Architects sit at the centre of it all without a deliberate data foundation, AI initiatives stall in pilot mode regardless of how strong the application layer becomes. This series brings the four perspectives into the same conversation, in the same sequence, so the architectural picture lines up across roles rather than being assembled in fragments later.

Who should attend?

This series is designed for professionals shaping the future of enterprise transformation in an AI-native world:

  • Enterprise Architects driving business and technology transformation
  • Solution and Platform Architects modernising SAP landscapes and integration strategies
  • Data and AI Architects designing the foundations for intelligent, autonomous operations
  • Technology and Transformation Leaders responsible for AI adoption and innovation strategy
  • SAP Customers and Partners exploring how to operationalise AI across enterprise landscapes
  • Architecture and Governance Teams focused on scalability, security, compliance, and lifecycle management
  • Innovation and Business Technology Teams moving from AI pilots to enterprise-wide adoption

As the role of the architect becomes increasingly multidimensional spanning business strategy, data, AI, governance, platforms, security, and human-centric transformation this series is built to help architects navigate and lead across these interconnected domains with confidence.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

What you will take away

By the end of the series, you will have:

  • A working mental model of how Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite, the Business AI Platform, Knowledge Graph, and the SAP Family of Models fit together architecturally
  • A hands-on reference for building, governing, and observing SAP agents across the enterprise
  • A clearer view of the data and governance foundations that determine whether AI scales or stalls
  • Customer-grounded perspectives from organisations including Bayer and Henkel on what is actually working in production
  • A holistic view of how the Enterprise Architect's role evolves when AI agents become first-class citizens within the enterprise landscape

We strongly recommend attending live where possible. The Q&A in each session is where the most useful exchanges tend to happen, and the cohort across the series will include architects from across the SAP ecosystem customers, partners, and SAP experts.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

Architects will define what the Autonomous Enterprise actually looks like in production. This series is built to make sure that definition is a deliberate one.

We look forward to seeing you in the sessions.

A Big Thank you to all the collaborators and my co-leads for this series!

 @JaSoN_Luo , @ralph_richter , @PaulKurchina @VenkataRaghu_Banda @helmut_grimm @johanneseuler

entry.title: A Look Back on my SAP Sapphire 2026 Experience
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire 2026 Orlando is officially in the books, and I am still riding the high from one of the most energizing weeks of the year. Between presenting the SAP HANA Cloud overview session and working the HANA Cloud booth, I had the pleasure to connect with hundreds of data professionals — answering questions, sparking conversations, and sharing potential database solutions. Here is a quick recap of what went down for me.

The Session: AI Starts with the Right Database

This overview presentation tackled two critical challenges that data teams are facing right now. First, the importance of bringing together every type and size of dataset in an organization — structured, unstructured, relational, graph, vector, spatial — in a way that actually makes AI smarter. Second, equipping data professionals with AI-driven tools so they can ship solutions faster without sacrificing quality.

As a single, cloud-native, in-memory database engine, SAP HANA Cloud supports analytics, applications, and AI from one platform. Its multi-model architecture means a singular database instance can simultaneously serve a transactional sales process, offer product recommendations using a vector engine, guide the customer experience using a knowledge graph, and increase operational efficiency thanks to spatial awareness. Additionally, the newly released SAP HANA Cloud agents reduce the SQL complexity and due diligence time that typically slows development teams down, enabling natural language access to database objects and relationships through an autogenerated knowledge graph.

The SAP HANA Cloud Booth: Four Questions That Kept Coming Back

Working the HANA Cloud booth proved just as rewarding as the session. Four questions surfaced repeatedly, and they are worth sharing here.

  • What is SAP Business Data Cloud? SAP Business Data Cloud is a suite of data management solutions — including SAP Datasphere, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Business Warehouse, and SAP Databricks — that work together to enable a business data fabric. Customers purchase BDC capacity units and apply them across these solutions based on their needs.
  • What does it mean that SAP HANA Cloud is now in SAP Business Data Cloud? HANA Cloud is an in-memory, multi-tiered, multi-model database capable of powering transactional applications, analytics, data science workflows, and AI use cases. Its inclusion in Business Data Cloud means customers now have access to a complete, unified set of data solutions.
  • How is SAP S/4HANA different from SAP HANA Cloud? S/4HANA is an ERP cloud solution built on a version of the HANA database. HANA Cloud is a standalone database that customers provision to power their own custom applications, analytics, or AI solutions.
  • Do I need SAP Business Data Cloud for AI? Absolutely. The effectiveness of any AI solution is directly tied to the quality, quantity, and quickness of the data behind it. SAP Business Data Cloud weaves together all data — SAP and non-SAP — into a singular business data fabric that is ready for Joule and other AI solutions. From a bigger picture perspective, the three domains of applications, data, and AI are intertwining so tightly that any one cannot exist effectively without the other two.

Keep the Momentum Going

If SAP Sapphire sparked your curiosity, do not let it stop there. Explore the free SAP HANA Cloud basic trial or the free SAP Business Data Cloud trial to get hands-on experience with these data solutions.

Have a topic about SAP HANA Cloud that is still fuzzy? Join the Data Professionals Community to learn, share, and grow with other data enthusiasts. Then, drop that question in the comments or create a discussion post — the conversation is just getting started.

entry.title: New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live
entry.content:
The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey is now live on learning.sap and alongside it the new role-play based SAP Certified Data Architect certification is now available in this learning Journey: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect

Early in my career, I sat in a room full of architects debating a major platform decision. The conversation covered integration patterns, application landscapes, and total cost of ownership. No one discussed the data. Not the quality of it, not how it would flow, not how it would be governed. We treated data as the responsibility of someone else (master data team and BW team) in a different meeting. Years later, we are seeing AI initiatives slow down because of untrustworthy data, I understand exactly what was missing from that room.

Data architecture has spent years as a supporting discipline important, but rarely the headline. That has changed. The shift to agentic AI has moved data architecture from a back-office concern to a front-line competitive capability.

Agentic AI systems do not simply retrieve information. They reason, plan, and act. They require data that is real-time, contextually grounded, semantically rich, and governed with precision. An agent operating on stale, fragmented, or poorly modelled data does not just produce mediocre outputs it can produce incorrect actions. At enterprise scale, that is a governance and business risk problem, not just a technical one.

The architects who will lead in this environment are those who can hold both conversations at once: the strategic (how data drives business value) and the technical (how to design the platforms, integration patterns, and governance structures that make that value real).

That is exactly what we have tried to address with the New Data Architecture learning Course (Advanced) and a New Data Architect Certification from SAP

Introducing the Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Course

 

 

The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey (course BDCDA2) is now live on learning.sap.com. Some of you might have done ‘Becoming a Data Architect – Foundational Course’ which was launched last year and this new course takes a deeper look at the topics related to Data Architecture.

Across four-unit journey designed for architects who are ready to move beyond foundational concepts and into the architecture decisions, framework synthesis, and platform-specific depth that define effective data leadership in the agentic AI era.

  1.  Unit 1: Architecting Data Strategy for the Agentic AI Era

The journey opens with strategy, because that is where most data initiatives fail first. Unit 1 builds the five foundational pillars of data strategy: Governance, Data literacy, Stewardship, Architecture, and Technology enablement and connects each directly to AI readiness. It examines what agentic AI demands from data pipelines that traditional analytics architectures were not built to provide: real-time streaming, semantic enrichment, and contextual grounding. It then moves to leadership and culture, the organizational factors that determine whether a data strategy survives contact with reality and closes with a framework for measuring data strategy success and analyzing real-world value patterns.

  1. Unit 2: Integrating Frameworks for Data Architecture

Architecture without a framework produces inconsistency. Unit 2 examines the three frameworks that define the professional standard in this field: TOGAF, DAMA-DMBOK, and the SAP Enterprise Architecture Framework and, guides how to synthesize them rather than apply them in isolation. It then applies that synthesis to two of the most significant architectural paradigms in the market today: Data Mesh and Data Fabric.

  1.  Unit 3: Harmonizing SAP Business Data Cloud Within the Open Data Ecosystem

Unit 3 is where strategy and frameworks meet the SAP platform. It begins with BDC Connect and the Delta Sharing protocol the mechanism that enables zero-copy data sharing across ecosystem boundaries and then works through each major integration scenario in the open data ecosystem: Databricks, Snowflake (including all three connectivity options), Microsoft Fabric, and Google Big Query. The unit closes with a dedicated lesson on data federation performance optimization the practical trade-offs between federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing that every architect advising on SAP Business Data Cloud deployments needs to understand cold.

  1. Unit 4: Designing Advanced Data Models and Architecture for SAP Business Data Cloud

The final unit addresses SAP Business Data Cloud as a Business Data Fabric how it implements federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing to connect heterogeneous data sources while preserving business context and semantic coherence. It covers Data Mesh in SAP BDC, including how Datasphere Spaces implement the four Data Mesh principles in practice. It then moves to one of the most consequential topics in enterprise AI architecture: semantic models and knowledge graphs as the foundation for trustworthy AI and RAG-based systems.

 Introducing the SAP Certified Data Architect Certification

Alongside the learning journey, we have launched the SAP Certified Data Architect certification.

The SAP Certified Data Architect is an AI role-play-based certification that assesses how well candidates understand modern data architecture in the context of strategic business success. It covers the five major architectural paradigms: Data Warehouse, Data Lake, Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Data Lakehouse, alongside the core elements of data maturity: governance frameworks, data security, and business-aligned data strategy. It uses interactive question-and-answer format, assessing the understanding of SAP Data Architecture in the agentic AI era specifically the ability to synthesize and articulate how SAP Business Data Cloud enables governed, semantically coherent data access across hybrid cloud environments.

 

Note: It is recommended that the candidate completes the 1) Becoming an SAP Data Architect and 2) Mastering SAP Data Architecture courses before attempting the certificate to ensure successful completion.

 Who This Is For

The learning journey and certification are designed for practitioners who are already working in the data and architecture space and want to develop the rigor, depth, and formal recognition to operate at senior level. That includes data architects designing enterprise data platforms, enterprise architects expanding their data architecture competency, analytics and BI leaders responsible for governed data access, and technical decision-makers advising customers on SAP Business Data Cloud strategy.

 If you are starting your data architecture journey, the foundational 'Becoming a Data Architect' learning journey on learning.sap.com is the right starting point. The advanced journey picks up where that one ends.

Getting Started

  1. The Becoming a Certified SAP Data Architect Learning Journey live now for both internal and external learners: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect
  2. SAP Certified Data Architect certification registration: https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-data-architect-sap-business-data-cloud

A Final Thought

In the agentic AI era, the constraint on enterprise intelligence is rarely the algorithm. It is the architecture underneath it the decisions made about how data is structured, governed, connected, and made meaningful. Those decisions require a specific set of skills. This learning journey and certification exist to build them.

The practitioners who invest in that depth now will be the ones designing the data foundations that AI-enabled enterprises run on. That work is not infrastructure. It is strategy.

entry.title: I Rebuilt My Home Server Three Times in Six Months. Here's What That Taught Me About Data Strategy.
entry.content:
Last Christmas, I Decided I Wanted a Home Server 🎄

Not because I had a grand plan, the need had crept up gradually. My wife Rachel had spent years building a real physical music library she cared about, and she wanted all of it digitized and accessible from one place, independent of a streaming service that might not carry it or a platform she didn't control. On my end I wanted somewhere to organize our media and actually back up our personal devices properly. Two pretty straightforward problems.

What followed wasn't a clean build. I started with the safe choice, a consumer-grade two-bay NAS with a clean interface and a reputation for just working. It hit its ceiling within weeks. So I pivoted to a more flexible open platform that could run containers, support a growing stack of self-hosted services, and actually feel like a lab instead of a fancy hard drive. Better, but still reactive. I was optimizing each step without ever mapping the full journey. By month five I finally did what I should have done at the start: sat down, defined what I actually wanted to build, and committed to a real foundation. Three hardware generations and a rebuilt container stack later, I had the homelab I should have built on day one. If I'd started with a strategy instead of a stopgap, I'd have gotten there in a fraction of the time.

I'm telling you this because I see the same pattern in enterprise data strategy more often than I'd like to admit.

🔄The Platform Refresh Problem

In enterprise technology, a platform refresh isn't just about new infrastructure. It's what happens when the system you chose at one level of organizational maturity can no longer perform at the next. Storage needs grow. Compute demands shift. Workloads that didn't exist on day one become mission-critical by year two. That's not failure, that's growth. But whether that transition is intentional or reactive determines how much rework, disruption, and lost value you absorb getting there.

Here's the thing: modern cloud-native data platforms are explicitly designed to make starting small feel like the right answer. Low friction, fast time-to-value, easy to justify to a stakeholder. And that instinct isn't wrong, it's a feature of those platforms, not a flaw in your thinking. The problem is that rapid business demand has a way of outpacing architecture before anyone realizes a plan was needed. The reporting layer that solved one team's urgent need quietly becomes the foundation for the enterprise's broader data strategy. Finance wants self-service. Operations wants real-time visibility. The business demands AI-ready pipelines. And suddenly the platform built for the first chapter of your data journey is being asked to perform in the third, without the architecture to support it.

The scramble that follows is disruptive, and it's almost always traceable back to an early decision made without sufficient long-term thinking. Not because anyone was careless, but because the platform made planning feel optional at the exact moment when momentum made starting feel urgent.

In BDC conversations with customers and prospects, I see this constantly. An organization starts with a tool that solves the immediate problem. The data strategy matures. The gap between where the architecture is and where the business needs to go becomes impossible to ignore. The question is never whether you'll need to evolve, it's whether that evolution will be intentional or forced.

🧭 Building Toward a Business Data Fabric

Don't scope your data platform to the problem you have today. Scope it to the data organization you're building toward.

Whether you're starting with a datamart, a departmental data warehouse, a data lake, a data lakehouse, or some combination of all of them, map the steps toward a business data fabric as your north star. That doesn't mean building it all at once. It means every decision you make today should be traceable to where you're going, not just where you are. A proper business data fabric integrates data across the enterprise, supports governed self-service, enables AI-ready workloads, and scales with the business rather than against it. That's the architecture worth planning toward from day one.

Ask the hard questions about integration, governance, scalability, and AI readiness before you're staring at a two-year-old architecture that can't do what the business now requires. A clear strategy upfront isn't a guarantee you'll never evolve, it means every evolution is intentional rather than reactive.

💡The Honest Takeaway

I rebuilt my home server three times because I kept optimizing each individual step without ever mapping the full journey first. Each decision was better than the last. Some were genuinely necessary. But the value lost to rework, rebuilding, and course-correcting under pressure never appeared on my original plan, it only showed up later, when the gap between where I was and where I needed to be had already gotten expensive to close.

That's the universal truth buried inside every technology implementation I've been part of. The most valuable path isn't the one with the lowest entry point, it's the one where you arrive at the right answer with the least waste. I started with nothing, made real mistakes, and eventually got there. But I got there faster once I stopped asking "what solves today's problem" and started asking "what gets me where I'm actually going."

One of the best parts of my job is helping customers do exactly that. There's a better path to your AI and data strategy, one that starts by defining the destination first. A business data fabric isn't just an architecture pattern. It's the north star that keeps every step of your journey pointed in the right direction. ⭐

🔗Explore SAP Business Data Fabric

▶️Watch a Demo

entry.title: Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise: A 6-City North America Tour for Architects
entry.content:
The announcements coming out of Sapphire this year were significant. Joule Work. The SAP Autonomous Suite. The SAP Business AI Platform. The SAP Family of Models. AI agents becoming first-class citizens inside enterprise landscapes.
 
For Enterprise Architects, this is not just another product update cycle. This is a fundamental shift in how businesses are designed, governed, and operated. And it is happening fast, faster than any of us can fully process alone.
 
That is why we are thrilled to announce that we are taking the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise experience on the road this summer for a complimentary 6-city in-person learning tour across North America.
 
CityDate
Palo Alto18 Jun 2026
Atlanta23 Jun 2026
Dallas25 Jun 2026
Toronto14 Jul 2026
Newtown Square16 Jul 2026
Calgary21 Jul 2026
 
👉 In-person tour registration: events.sap.com/eaa-aet/en_us/home.html

This is an architect-to-architect event. No sales pitches. Just practitioners, thought leaders, customers, and community working through what this era of transformation means in practice, together.

What We Will Explore Together

Each city event is structured around eight core sessions designed to take you from strategic clarity through to practical architectural guidance:
 
  1. SAP Sapphire Key Takeaways for Enterprise Architects : What Matters and Why We cut through the noise. What were the announcements that genuinely change the architectural picture, and what are the implications for how you plan, design, and govern your enterprise landscape going forward?
  2. Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise : What It Is, Why It Matters, and How to Prepare Before you can architect toward a destination, you need to understand what it actually is. We ground the vision of the Autonomous Enterprise in concrete architectural terms : what changes, what stays the same, and where the real decisions lie.
  3. Decoding SAP's Evolving Portfolio : Implications for Enterprise Architecture SAP's portfolio is evolving rapidly. This session maps the strategic shifts — from SAP Autonomous Suite to the Business AI Platform — and draws out the direct implications for enterprise architects responsible for modernising, integrating, and governing complex SAP landscapes.
  4. Designing the AI-Native Enterprise : Defining Your North Star Architecture Every transformation journey needs a destination. This session helps you define your North Star Architecture for the AI-native enterprise — a forward-looking reference model that guides investment decisions, platform choices, and integration patterns in an agentic world.
  5. The AI Golden Path : Developing AI Applications Across the SAP Ecosystem The Golden Path is how you move from strategy to execution. We explore the recommended patterns, platforms, and practices for developing, deploying, and scaling AI applications across the SAP ecosystem, giving you a repeatable approach that works at enterprise scale.
  6. Who Governs the Agents? AI Governance at Scale As AI agents proliferate across enterprise landscapes, governance becomes the defining architectural challenge. This session tackles agent lifecycle management, security, compliance, trust, and the guardrails that allow organisations to move fast without losing control.
  7. The Enterprise Architect's Role in the Autonomous Enterprise — Leadership, Influence, and Community The role of the Enterprise Architect is being redefined. This session explores how EAs position themselves not just as technical designers but as strategic leaders driving AI adoption, shaping governance frameworks, and influencing transformation at the board level.
  8. Architect-to-Architect Open Forum : What We're Excited About, What We're Missing, and What Comes Next This is the session I personally look forward to most at every stop. No slides. No script. Just an open, honest conversation between architects , about what is working, what is not, what the community is excited about, and what we still need to figure out together.

 Where We Are Heading

We will be visiting six cities across North America this summer. Each event is complimentary and designed to give you a full day of learning, connection, and conversation:
📍 Palo Alto — June 18, 2026
📍 Atlanta — June 23, 2026
📍 Dallas — June 25, 2026
📍 Toronto — July 14, 2026
📍 Newtown Square — July 16, 2026
📍 Calgary — July 21, 2026

Bonus Morning Sessions — Going Even Deeper

For those who want to go further, each city also features an optional complimentary morning learning experience before the main event:
 
🔐 Security, AI and Governance Across SAP Cloud Environments Available in: Palo Alto · Atlanta · Dallas · Calgary A focused deep dive into how organisations design and enforce security, privacy, compliance, and clean-core principles across SAP and non-SAP environments in an AI-native world.
 
📊 Data Inspired: Street-Smart Data Literacy Available in: Toronto · Newtown Square Inspired by Dr. Sebastian Wernicke's acclaimed book and TED Talk, this morning session brings practical data literacy to life for architects and transformation leaders — helping you ask better questions, make sharper decisions, and build organisations that genuinely run on data.
 
The pace of change around AI, Agentic AI, and the Autonomous Enterprise is accelerating faster than any of us can keep up with individually.
 
The architects we speak with every week are brilliant, curious, and deeply committed to leading their organisations through this transformation but they are often doing it in isolation, working through the same questions, hitting the same walls, solving the same problems independently. That has to change.
 
The best thinking will come from a conversation in a corridor, a whiteboard session that ran forty-five minutes over, a question from the back of the room that reframed everything.
 
That is what this tour is designed to create. Not just learning, but community feeling.
 
There has never been a more exciting time to be an Enterprise Architect. And there has never been a more important time to be connected to others who are navigating this journey alongside you.
 
For those who cannot join us in person, please feel free to join us online on our webinar series (see below).

Register Now for the In-person Experience — It Is Complimentary

Both the in-person tour and the online series are completely free to attend.  All you need to do is register.
 
👉 In-person tour registration: events.sap.com/eaa-aet/en_us/home.html

The Online Webinar Series - Register Below

If you cannot join us in person or if you want to go even deeper on the technical and architectural details we are also running a companion 11-part online webinar series, Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise, every week from 1st June through to 12th August 2026.
 
Covering Joule Work, the SAP Business AI Platform, AI Agent Lifecycle Management, the SAP Family of Models, Data Foundations, Governance, and much more — it is the same architect-to-architect spirit, delivered globally and available live and on-demand.
👉 Full series details and registration: events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html
 
I am looking forward to reconnecting with familiar faces and meeting new architects across our ecosystem. We would love to hear what you are working on, what is keeping you up at night, and where you think this all goes next.
 
Thank you & Regards,
 
Paul Kurchina, Ralph Richter & Gaurish Dessai

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in May
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in May

May was one of those months where everything happened at once. SAPPHIRE Orlando brought a wave of announcements for SAP Business Data Cloud — platform direction, new integrations, and some big bets on enterprise AI. And while the conference was still in full swing, the product team kept shipping: two solid Datasphere releases packed with useful enhancements for data integration, modeling, and transport.

Below you'll find my top feature picks from the May releases, a summary of the key SAPPHIRE announcements, and the usual roundup of community blogs worth reading.

 

 

My top features in May

SAP Snowflake and BDC Connect for Snowflake

Two closely related announcements that mark a big step for the BDC open partner ecosystem.

SAP Snowflake is now generally available as a solution extension — Snowflake's AI/ML and advanced data engineering capabilities delivered directly through SAP. Full Snowflake Business Critical Edition, provisioned via SAP for Me, with zero-copy access to SAP data products out of the box.

BDC Connect for Snowflake adds bidirectional data sharing on AWS for customers already running Snowflake. Same enrollment approach as BDC Connect for Databricks — data products flow in both directions, business semantics and governance intact.

Run Replication Flows with Delta Load at Scheduled Times

Long-awaited. You can now schedule replication flows that use delta-enabled objects by setting Delta Load Run to "At Scheduled Time". The flow processes all available delta records and completes cleanly after each run. No more workarounds.

Bulk Assignment of Schedule Owner for Task Chains

You can now assign a schedule owner to multiple task chains at once — no more one-by-one configuration. A small change with a big impact for anyone managing larger environments with many scheduled jobs.

Analyze the Lineage Graph of a View with Runtime Metrics

The Runtime Metrics tool now lets you analyze the full lineage graph of a view, including all underlying source objects — not just the view in isolation. Much clearer visibility into where performance bottlenecks actually live.

Transport Runtime Settings for Flows and Views

Transformation flow runtime settings — HANA run modes, batch configurations, Spark application settings — can now be included in transport packages. The same applies to view persistency runtime settings in the Data Integration Monitor. One transport, consistent behavior across dev, test, and production.

Variables in Analytic Models

When using an analytic model as a source for another analytic model, you can now customize its variables — rename them, change default values, use them in restricted measures and calculations in the consuming model. A real flexibility gain for anyone building layered analytic models.

SAP Analytics Cloud Q2 2026 — Top 5 Features

A quick look at the QRC2 2026 SAC release Orla_C put together a great video walkthrough covering the top 5:

  • Asymmetric Reporting
  • Composite Versioning
  • Job Monitor Enhancements
  • Decoupled Data Panel
  • Recent & Favorites in open/save dialogs

➡️  Watch it here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0U7xc_8WN2I

 
 

 

SAPPHIRE 2026

My personal highlight at SAPPHIRE Orlando 🌴

This year I had the privilege of opening the SAP Business Data Cloud track at SAPPHIRE Orlando with the introductory session on Monday — and the response was something I didn't expect.

The session ended up as the #1 most registered session at SAPPHIRE 2026 overall, with more than 1,800 sign-ups over two sessions. And a lot of them showed up in the room. I'm still a bit speechless. Humbled, and honestly — kind of proud.

Thank you to everyone who registered, attended, or just followed along from afar. The energy in that room made it feel like more than a tech talk.

 

What's New in SAP Business Data Cloud at SAPPHIRE

SAPPHIRE 2026 brought a wave of announcements for SAP Business Data Cloud. Here's a quick summary of what was announced.

SAP Business AI Platform — SAP BTP, SAP Business Data Cloud, and SAP Business Transformation Management are converging into a single unified platform for building, contextualizing, and governing enterprise AI. At its core sits the SAP Knowledge Graph, which maps business entities, processes, and relationships across your SAP landscape.

SAP HANA Cloud natively in BDC — HANA Cloud becomes a core component of BDC, adding a unified in-memory engine for transactional, analytical, and multi-model workloads — alongside the existing SAP Databricks and SAP Snowflake integrations.

MDG and Reltio in BDC — SAP Master Data Governance is now a component of BDC, ensuring data products are verified and governance-aligned. The Reltio acquisition is complete and will be brought directly into BDC as well, adding multi-domain master data management with AI-based entity resolution and Model Context Protocol support for multi-agent workflows.

Joule Agents in the data fabric — Data products, analytical models, and planning structures can be created through natural language. SAP Analytics Cloud story generation is part of this too.

BDC Connect for Amazon Athena — Bi-directional zero-copy integration between BDC and Amazon Athena, extending the existing Snowflake and Databricks integrations. General availability planned for H2 2026.

👉I covered all of this in a dedicated recap video 

 

My favorite blogs in May

@GregBotticchio covers a genuinely useful new feature in SAP Analytics Cloud Q2 2026: the Asymmetric Reporting Layout lets you design tables where different sections follow different structures — actuals at monthly level, forecast from a cut-off date, budget at quarterly level — all in a single table, no workarounds, no duplicated objects. If you've ever built three separate tables just to answer one business question, this one's for you.
🔗 Asymmetric Reporting Layout

@yoramfriedman breaks down a structural problem that doesn't get talked about enough: the place where business context lives can't run agents, and the place that can run agents doesn't have the context. Using S/4HANA as the example, he shows how meaning — contract terms, credit history, priority rules, semantic layers — stays behind when you extract data to a generic lake. A sharp read that explains the core problem SAP Business Data Cloud is designed to solve.
🔗 The Catch-22 of Most Business AI

@Gaurish-Dessai announces something worth knowing about: a new advanced learning journey and certification for SAP Data Architects, covering data strategy for the agentic AI era, architectural frameworks like TOGAF and DAMA-DMBOK, and SAP BDC integrations with Databricks, Snowflake, Fabric, and BigQuery. If data architecture is your game, this is your next step.
🔗 New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live

@cecilysorenson covers the latest Discovery Center updates — including a dedicated SAP BDC Service Catalog with missions, roadmaps, and transparent pricing, plus an AI Maturity Calculator developed with IDC. One central place to go from initial BDC exploration to full adoption, with practical guidance for managing SAP and non-SAP data.
🔗 What's New in SAP Discovery Center: From Inspiration to Guidance

@kurzdo93 traces the evolution of enterprise data architecture from the data warehouse era through the Lakehouse to what's needed today: a knowledge core that gives AI agents the business context to actually act, not just answer. He uses SAP Business Data Cloud to show how lake storage, intelligent compute (HANA Cloud, Databricks, Snowflake), semantic data products, and Joule agents come together as a business data fabric. A well-structured read if you want to understand the architectural "why" behind BDC.
🔗 AI has outgrown legacy data architectures like SAP BW — is your stack ready to keep up?



More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

  • SAP Snowflake: How to Store and Manage Secrets for APIs, Database Credentials, and Keys

entry.title: SAP Datasphere: Data Tiering Strategy and Best Practices
entry.content:
Introduction:

Data tiering is becoming a critical pillar of every organization’s data and analytics strategy. As companies move toward AI-driven decision-making and deploy AI agents, MCP servers, and other autonomous capabilities, the value of context-rich, trusted data products increases dramatically. Simply put: high‑quality data is the fuel that powers generative, agentic, and autonomous applications across the business.A modern data tiering (or data management) strategy is about organizing and storing data in a way that optimizes both cost and performance. Data is being generated at unprecedented speed and volume, from business transactions and master data to IoT signals, application logs, surveys, social media, and streaming sources. Not all of this data is equally valuable at all times, and not all of it needs the same level of performance or freshness. The key is to balance the cost of storing data with the cost (and business value) of accessing it.

Different consumption patterns drive different requirements:

  • Real-time operational analytics (daily, weekly)
  • Tactical reporting (monthly, quarterly)
  • Strategic analysis (multi-year historical trends)
  • Advanced AI and machine learning use cases

For each of these, it is essential to understand what data tiering is, which options exist, and in which scenarios to apply which tier. In this technical article, we focus on data tiering in the context of SAP’s Business Data Cloud, with a particular emphasis on SAP Datasphere. We align the discussion with SAP’s latest data and analytics strategy as presented at Sapphire 2026, and provide practical guidance and best practices for designing an effective tiering strategy that supports both traditional analytics and next-generation AI workloads.

1. Business & AI Context:

AI is no longer an experimental add‑on to the SAP landscape; it is becoming the execution layer of the enterprise. With SAP’s vision of the Autonomous Enterprise, AI agents, assistants, and Joule-powered workflows are expected to automate large parts of finance, supply chain, HR, and customer operations. All of that depends on one thing: trusted, well‑managed data with rich business context.

1.1 Why Data Tiering Matters in an AI-First World?

Modern AI in SAP is context-hungry. It does not just need a lot of data; it needs: Who the customer  is across CRM, S/4HANA, and e‑commerce, Which material or location a transaction relates to, What policies or controls apply in a given process or region, How a process (order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, hire-to-retire) is supposed to run. This business context is what SAP brings to AI, and it is exactly what SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Datasphere are designed to provide. However, the data feeding AI agents, planning models, and analytics is exploding in volume and variety:

  • Core ERP transactions and master data
  • IoT, machine telemetry, and sensor streams
  •  Application logs and clickstreams
  • Survey, social, and interaction data
  • External market and partner data

Not all of this data needs to sit on the most expensive, highest‑performance storage. At the same time, some scenarios, like Joule agents making operational decisions, cannot tolerate high latency or missing data. Data tiering is how you manage this trade‑off between cost and performance without compromising AI quality and trust.

1.2 From Reporting to Autonomous Decisions

Historically, data tiering conversations in SAP environments focused on:

  • Reducing database size (especially in ERP and BW)
  • Managing performance for reporting and planning
  • Meeting retention and compliance requirements at lower cost

In the Autonomous Enterprise, the stakes are higher. Tiering decisions now influence:

  • Real-time operational analytics: e.g., S/4HANA operational KPIs, supply chain control towers, same‑day profitability
  • Tactical management reporting: month‑end/quarter‑end analysis, scenario planning, rolling forecasts
  • Strategic and historical analysis: multi‑year trends, structural cost analysis, sustainability reporting
  • AI and agentic use cases: Joule agents executing workflows, AI copilots assisting users in SAP Fiori and line-of-business apps,RAG‑style (retrieval‑augmented generation) scenarios pulling context into LLMs, ML models trained on long‑range historical, IoT, or behavioral data

Each of these has a different tolerance for latency, freshness, data volume, and cost. A well‑designed tiering strategy ensures that:

  • Hot data is available at in‑memory speed for agents and operational analytics
